coredata.dc:title: Biodegradable and photostable Nb2C MXene quantum dots as promising nanofluorophores for metal ions sensing and fluorescence imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Guanghui
Zhao, Jinlai
Yi, Shaozhen
Wan, Xuejuan
Tang, Jiaoning
coredata.dc:description: Conventional fluorescent probes are either easily photobleached or non-biodegradable, which often leads to the unstable fluorescence signal output and long-term biological toxicity. Therefore, the development of novel fluorescent materials with both excellent photostability and biodegradability is of great significance for further broadening their application in numerous research fields. In this work, Nb2C quantum dots (Nb2C QDs) with pristine crystallographic structures of Nb2C MXene phases and surface oxygen-containing species are synthesized by an ultrasound assisted physicochemical exfoliation in tetrapropylammonium hydroxide. Detailed analyses indicate that the Nb2C QDs not only possess excellent chemical- and photo-stable fluorescence emission but also achieve successful application in fluorescence sensing of heavy metal ions and fluorescence imaging. More importantly, it is confirmed that the Nb2C QDs present high biocompatibility and unique biodegradation property responsive to human myeloperoxidase, implying the application safety of Nb2C QDs in vivo. Under the background of ever-growing and stringent requirements for biosafety and technical stability, the chemical- and photo-stability, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and most importantly the promising fluorescence sensing/imaging characteristic of the obtained Nb2C QDs may argue well for their future applications in environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnosis, visual display, and anti-counterfeiting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: MXene quantum dots
Fluorescent probes
Biodegradation
Photobleaching resistance
Fluorescence
Imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Nb2C QDs
Potential of Nb2C QDs as fluorescent probes
Photostability and biodegradability of Nb2C QDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21802097 , 51778369 , 21875144 ) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2018A030313758 , 2017A030310324 , 2017A030313047 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) AFM image and (b) height profiles of the as-prepared Nb2C QDs. (c) TEM image of Nb2C QDs (inset shows the corresponding lateral size distribution histogram). (d) HRTEM image and the corresponding FFT pattern (inset) of Nb2C QDs. (e) XRD spectra of Nb2AlC bulk and Nb2C QDs. (f) Raman spectra of Nb2AlC bulk and Nb2C QDs.

(a) FTIR spectra of Nb2AlC bulk and Nb2C QDs. (b) ζ-Potential spectrum of the as-prepared Nb2C QDs suspension. (c) XPS survey spectra of Nb2AlC bulk and Nb2C QDs. (d), (e) and (f) The high-resolution XPS spectra of the Nb 3d, O 1s and C 1s peaks of Nb2C QDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption, PL excitation (Ex, λem =485 nm) and PL emission (Em, λex =370 nm) spectra of Nb2C QDs in aqueous solutions. (b) Normalized emission spectra of Nb2C QDs aqueous solutions excited under different wavelengths from 330 nm to 450 nm.

(a) PL emission spectra of Nb2C QDs dispersed in different pH solutions. (b) Digital photographs of Nb2C QDs solutions with different pH values. (c) The reversible PL intensity of Nb2C QDs solution when the pH value alternates between 2 and 11.

(a) PL emission spectra of Nb2C QDs solutions with the addition of different metal ions (1 mM) and (b) the digital photographs of the corresponding mixture under 360 nm UV light. (c) Relative PL intensities of Nb2C QDs solutions with the addition of competing metal ions (1 mM, red bar) and the following addition of 1 mM of Fe3+ (black bar). (d) PL emission spectra of Nb2C QDs solutions with the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+. (e) The relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentrations, with the inset showing the linear range between 0 and 0.3 mM (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article). (For interpretation of the references to colour in the Figure, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Relative viabilities of NIH 3T3 cells after being incubated with various concentrations of Nb2C QDs for 12 and 24 h. (b), (c) and (d) The CLSM fluorescence images of NIH 3T3 cells incubated with 100, 150 and 200 μg mL−1 of Nb2C QDs, respectively. Photographs of round (e), linear (f) and square (g) PVA/Nb2C QDs hydrogels. Bright-field images (e1, f1, g1) and the corresponding fluorescent pseudo-colored images (e2, f2, g2) of mice with the PVA/Nb2C QDs hydrogels implanted subcutaneously on their back.

(a) PL emission spectra and (b) the corresponding normalized PL intensity of Nb2C QDs, FITC and RhB aqueous solutions irradiated by a 350 W xenon lamp for different times. Inset in (b) shows the fluorescence photographs of the three solutions before and 40 min after xenon lamp irradiation.

(a) Photographs, (b) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (c) PL emission spectra of Nb2C QDs after receiving various biodegradation treatments for 24 h. (d), (e) and (f) are the AFM images and the corresponding height profiles of Nb2C QDs after receiving various biodegradation treatments for 24 h.

head.ce:title: Biodegradable and photostable Nb2C MXene quantum dots as promising nanofluorophores for metal ions sensing and fluorescence imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology, Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology
Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shenzhen Key Laboratory of Polymer Science and Technology Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Conventional fluorescent probes are either easily photobleached or non-biodegradable, which often leads to the unstable fluorescence signal output and long-term biological toxicity. Therefore, the development of novel fluorescent materials with both excellent photostability and biodegradability is of great significance for further broadening their application in numerous research fields. In this work, Nb2C quantum dots (Nb2C QDs) with pristine crystallographic structures of Nb2C MXene phases and surface oxygen-containing species are synthesized by an ultrasound assisted physicochemical exfoliation in tetrapropylammonium hydroxide. Detailed analyses indicate that the Nb2C QDs not only possess excellent chemical- and photo-stable fluorescence emission but also achieve successful application in fluorescence sensing of heavy metal ions and fluorescence imaging. More importantly, it is confirmed that the Nb2C QDs present high biocompatibility and unique biodegradation property responsive to human myeloperoxidase, implying the application safety of Nb2C QDs in vivo. Under the background of ever-growing and stringent requirements for biosafety and technical stability, the chemical- and photo-stability, biocompatibility, biodegradability, and most importantly the promising fluorescence sensing/imaging characteristic of the obtained Nb2C QDs may argue well for their future applications in environmental monitoring, biomedical diagnosis, visual display, and anti-counterfeiting.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes have become the most powerful tools in sensing and imaging because of their high selectivity, sensitivity, fast response, and low cost [1–4]. In the past decades, scholars have made significant progress in developing fluorescent probes that respond to various micro-changes (e.g., pH value, and concentration of ions and/or molecules). Conventional fluorescent probe materials can be roughly classified into organic fluorophores and inorganic nanofluorophores. Organic fluorophores such as organic dyes are the earliest fluorescent probe materials to be studied and applied in practice [5]. However, due to their intrinsic disadvantages including poor biocompatibility, and especially rapid photobleaching and photodegradation, organic fluorophores have been greatly limited in the application of long-term fluorescence sensing and imaging [6,7]. Compared with organic fluorophores, mostly reported inorganic nanofluorophores such as semiconductor quantum dots, metal nanoclusters and carbon/graphene nanodots have excellent photobleaching resistance [8–11]. In addition, through simple chemical modification, these inorganic fluorophores also show good biocompatibility. These advantages endow inorganic nanofluorophores with good application potential in sensing and imaging, which makes them have attracted great attention in recent years. Unfortunately, however, a non-negligible drawback of these inorganic nanofluorophores is that they are usually non-biodegradable [12]. When used for sensing or imaging in vivo, these probes are easily enriched in tissues or organs and eventually lead to long-term toxicity [13,14]. Therefore, the development of new fluorescent probes with both excellent photostability and biodegradability is of great significance for further widening the application of this important tool.

Over the past 8 years, 2D early transition-metal carbides (MXenes) nanosheets (lateral dimension from hundreds of nanometers to several micrometers) have been attracting an explosion of interest due to their ultrathin thickness (molecular or atomic level) and unique physicochemical performances such as excellent metallic conductivity, chemical stability and hydrophilicity [15–20]. The superior properties of MXenes nanosheets distinguish them from traditional nanomaterials and make them promising potential for a variety of extraordinary applications such as nanoelectronics, supercapacitors, ultrafast photonics and catalysis [21–23]. Although MXenes nanosheets have also shown potential applications in electrochemical sensing, few studies focus on their fluorescence sensing and/or imaging, as MXenes nanosheets usually have little fluorescence [24,25].

Quite recently, both Professor Zhi's and Professor Geng's groups found that by further reducing the lateral dimension of MXenes nanosheets to several nanometers to form quantum dots (QDs), the newly obtained MXenes QDs exhibited unexpectedly bright fluorescence, due to the fact that the electronic and optical properties of these materials were modified by quantum confinement and edge effects [26,27]. These discoveries open another door for the application of MXenes, that is, MXenes QDs have the potential to be used as fluorescent probes. However, only a few studies have been carried out to applying Ti3C2 MXenes QDs (derived from the most widely studied Ti3C2 MXenes) in fluorescence sensing and imaging since 2017, and afterwards no other MXene QDs are reported [28,29]. Most importantly, up to now, no studies have focused on the biodegradability and photobleaching resistance of any MXenes QDs. Therefore, we speculated that the exploration and extension of MXenes QDs family, accompanied by the systematic investigation of their chemical stability, biocompatibility, photobleaching resistance and biodegradability, will make sense to their further expansion on diverse application field, which is of great significance and full of challenge.

In this work, we fabricate a novel MXene (Nb2C) QD by simultaneous interlayer delamination and layer cutting through a high-intensity ultrasonication treatment in tetrapropylammonium hydroxide (TPAOH). The obtained Nb2C QDs with well-defined structures and abundant functional groups exhibit bright and excitation-dependent fluorescence. The application of Nb2C QDs as fluorescent probes is demonstrated through their selective fluorescence sensing of Fe3+ and imaging of cells and hydrogel implants. More importantly, further systematic study confirms that these biocompatible Nb2C QDs feature both amazing enzyme-responsive biodegradability and excellent photobleaching resistance, which is evident for their potential application in fluorescence sensing and imaging.

Nb2AlC powders (98 % purity, 200 mesh) were purchased from Forsman Scientific Co., Ltd (China). HF solution (47–51 %) and TPAOH (25 wt% aqueous solution) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Myeloperoxidase from human neutrophils (hMPO) were obtained from Athens Research and Technology Inc. (USA). All other chemicals were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (China) and used without further purification. Ultrapure water used throughout the experiments was obtained from a Milli-Q millipore system.

Nb2C QDs were prepared by a pulsed ultrasound assisted physicochemical exfoliation in TPAOH. Briefly, 5 g of Nb2AlC powder was immersed and intensely stirred in 50 mL HF solution for 2 days to selectively etch the Al element. Then, the resulting suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove the excess HF. The collected Nb2C stacks were centrifugally washed with ethanol until the pH reached above 6. The wet stacks were then stirred in 60 mL TPAOH aqueous solution, accompanied with a high-intensity pulsed ultrasonication treatment for 10 h (Fisher Scientific model 505 sonic dismembrator). An ice bath was used to keep the temperature of the reaction system below room temperature. During this process, the combined interaction of the chemical reaction between TPAOH and Nb2C and the physical shearing forces from the ultrasound led to perpendicular cleavage and in-plane fragmentation of the Nb2C stacks. After that, the mixture was filtered with a 0.22 μM filter membrane to remove the presence of any big particles. After adjusting its pH to about 7 with HNO3, the yellow filtrate was dialyzed for 5 days to remove the residual TPAOH. Finally, the targeted Nb2C QDs were obtained by freeze drying the purified filtrate.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) analysis was conducted on a Bruker Dimension Icon microscope. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) investigations were operated on a FEI Tecnai G2 F30. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on a Nicolet 6700 FTIR spectrometer. An Olympus microscope coupled Renishaw Raman spectrometer was used for Raman analysis. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were acquired on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD X-ray electron spectrometer with focused monochromatized Al Kα radiation (15 kV). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm). UV–vis absorption measurement was conducted on a Shimadzu UV-2450 spectrometer. Fluorescence spectra were recorded on a SANCO970CRT spectrometer using 2/2 nm slit width. The fluorescent lifetime was measured by Horiba Jobin Yvon DeltaFlex equipped with a NanoLED-390 excitation source. ζ-potential and particle size distribution were measured by a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90. The confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) images were acquired on an Olympus FV1000 microscope.

The selectivity of Nb2C QDs was explored with 15 common metal ions. Test solutions were prepared by placing 5 mL of Nb2C QDs solution (2 mg mL−1) into a tube, adding 100 μL of the respective metal ion stock solution (0.1 M) and diluting the mixture to 10 mL. After shaking the mixture for a minute, their fluorescence spectra were recorded. The anti-interference ability of Nb2C QDs for Fe3+ sensing was studied by simultaneously adding Fe3+ and interfering ions at a molar ratio of 1:1. The sensitivity of Nb2C QDs to Fe3+ was measured via the same procedure by adding 100 μL of stock solution with different Fe3+ concentration.

The cytotoxicity of Nb2C QDs was determined by the standard MTT assay with NIH 3T3 fibroblast cells. NIH 3T3 cells were seeded into 96-well plates (1 × 104 cells well−1) for 24 h at 37 °C. Then Nb2C QDs with various concentrations were separately introduced to the plates followed by incubating for another 12 or 24 h at 37 °C. 20 μL of 0.5 % MTT was added to co-incubate with cells for 4 h, after washing 3 times with PBS, 100 μL DMSO was then added to resolve the intracellular MTT. Finally, the optical density value (OD) of each well was examined at 490 nm by using a microplate reader.

For cell imaging testing, the NIH 3T3 cells at the logarithmic growth phase were seeded into glass bottom confocal dishes (1 × 105 cells) and cultured in 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for 24 h. The freeze-dried Nb2C QDs powder was redispersed into DMEM medium as stocking medium. The stocking medium was then added to co-incubate with cells under the same conditions. After 4 h incubation, cells were washed with PBS for 3 times to remove excessive samples, and then the treated cells were observed by Olympus. To assess the in vivo imaging capacity of Nb2C QDs, a small animal in vivo fluorescence imaging system (Maestro EX-Pro, USA) was used to explore the fluorescent images (Pseudo- colour images; Ex, Em and exposure time were set as 590 nm, 700 nm and 20 s, respectively) of PVA/Nb2C QDs hydrogels implanted subcutaneously into the back of mice. The PVA/Nb2C QDs composite hydrogels were prepared by a freezing/thawing approach reported in our previous work [8]. The Kunming mice aged 8 weeks and weighing about 25 g were used for this study. All the animal procedures were performed strictly in compliance with the requirements and guidelines of the Laboratory and Equipment Department and Institutional Ethical Committee of Animal Experimentation of Health Science Center, Shenzhen University.

140 mM NaCl, 100 μM diethylene triamine pentaacetic acid, 250 μg Nb2C QDs and 50 μg hMPO were suspended in 500 μL of 50 mM phosphate buffer. Hydrogen peroxide was added at a rate of 100 mM per hour for 5 h and the incubation system was maintained at 37 °C for 24 h. Due to the loss of activity of hMPO in the mixture, the enzyme was replenished every 5 h during the degradation process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of Nb2C QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Nb2C QDs were synthesized through a pulsed ultrasound assisted chemical exfoliation in TPAOH, as described in the experimental section. The morphology and chemical structure of the as-prepared Nb2C QDs were analyzed via AFM, TEM, XRD and Raman spectra. Fig. 1
a and Fig. 1b show the AFM image and the corresponding height profiles of the Nb2C QDs, respectively. It is evident that Nb2C QDs are highly dispersed with a thickness of about 1.2 nm, indicating the formation of bilayer Nb2C QDs. The HR-TEM image and size distribution analysis in Fig. 1c demonstrate that the Nb2C QDs are uniform ultrasmall particles with lateral sizes ranging from 1 to 5 nm. The lattice fringes of Nb2C QDs with an inner-plane spacing of 0.24 nm is clearly visible (Fig. 1d), and the fast Fourier transform pattern can also confirm their hexagonal crystalline structure, which is in accordance with those of pristine Nb2C MXenes nanosheets [6]. Meanwhile, Nb2C QDs show no obvious peak in the XRD pattern except for a broad halo (Fig. 1e). Similar XRD patterns have been observed for other MXenes materials, which imply the highly delaminated nature of the MXenes nanostructures [26]. Comparing to the pristine Nb2AlC bulks, the Raman vibration modes of the Nb2C QDs exhibit significant changes in Fig. 1f, i.e. the disappearance of vibration modes ω1, ω3, and the decreasing of ω2, ω4, which indicate the elimination of the Al layer and the preservation of Nb2C layer as literature reported [12,30]. All the above results manifest the successful synthesis of Nb2C QDs, and the obtained material exhibit pristine crystallographic structure of Nb2C MXene with ultrasmall lateral size and good dispersibility.

The surface chemical compositions of Nb2C QDs were determined by FTIR, ζ-potential and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the FTIR spectrum of Nb2C QDs displays stretching vibrations corresponding to −OH at 3425 cm−1, CO at 1640 cm −1 and CF at 1100 cm−
1 [27,31]. The ζ-potential measurement shows a negative value at -34.3 mV (Fig. 2b), which can be attributed to the negative moieties on the surface of Nb2C QDs. The XPS survey spectrum of Nb2C QDs in Fig. 2c shows the presence of F and the disappearance of Al, evidencing the complete elimination of the Al layer in Nb2AlC. High-resolution XPS spectra of Nb2C QDs in the Nb 3d, O 1s and C 1s regions were fitted and then shown in Fig. 2d-f, respectively. The Nb 3d XPS spectrum is deconvoluted into three prominent bands centered at 206.2, 207.7 and 210.4 eV, which can be assigned to Nb–C, NbO and Nb2O5, respectively [22,32]. From the O 1s spectrum, bands centered at 531.8, 531.0 and 530.1 eV represent the O 1s states in C–Nb–Ox I, C–Nb–(OH)x II, and Nb2O5, respectively. The C 1s region is deconvoluted into three peaks at 286.8, 284.9 and 282.6 eV, corresponding to CHx, CC and Nb–C bonds, respectively. Accordingly, it can be concluded from the FTIR and XPS results that there are oxygen-containing species on the surface of Nb2C QDs, which may endow them with metal ion sensing capacity similar to carbon quantum dots.

The ultraviolet − visible (UV − Vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of Nb2C QDs suspension were recorded in Fig. 3
a. Similarly to other reported ultrasmall nanosheets such as Ti3C2 and MnO2, [27,33,34] the UV–vis spectrum below 400 nm increases significantly with the decrease of wavelength, whereas no obvious peaks are observed over the whole wavelength range from 200 to 700 nm. This spectral feature is mostly attributed to the size effect of Nb2C QDs with such an ultrasmall lateral dimension, that is, the position of the absorption edge of Nb2C QDs blue-shifts with the decrease of its dimension. As shown in Fig. 3a, Nb2C QDs exhibit maximal PL excitation peak and corresponding PL emission peak at 370 nm and 485 nm, respectively, and the light yellow Nb2C QDs suspension emits a strong bluish green fluorescence under the excitation of 365 nm UV light. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield of Nb2C QDs is determined to be 8.4 % (Figure S1). Fig. 3b evidences that Nb2C QDs display an excitation-dependent emission property, where the PL emission peaks red-shifts from 480 to 540 nm with increasing the excitation wavelength from 330 to 450 nm. Although the PL mechanism and excitation-dependent PL behavior of QDs is awaiting verification, it is generally believed that these properties can be mainly attributed to the surface defects and size effects [35–37]. As for our prepared Nb2C QDs, their bright PL emission may be ascribed to the strong quantum confinement resulted from the ultrasmall lateral dimension, and the surface defects caused by the oxygen-containing species.

In addition, the pH effect on the fluorescence stability of Nb2C QDs was investigated and illustrated in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows no obvious change of fluorescence intensity within the pH range from 5 to 11, whereas the intensity decreases dramatically at pH below 5. The apparent stability of Nb2C QDs suspensions with different pH values shows a similar trend. As shown in Fig. 4b, when the pH is below 5, the suspensions become turbid, and the Nb2C QDs precipitate significantly after only 4 min. On contrary, the Nb2C QDs suspensions with pH above 5 consistently remain clear and transparent, and no sedimentation is observed. This phenomenon may be attributed to the deprotonation/protonation effects of Nb2C QDs [10]. When the pH value is above 5, the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of Nb2C QDs are sufficiently deprotonated, which leads to a high net surface negative charge. In this way, the suspensions could keep stable due to the electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged Nb2C QDs, and consequently cause no deterioration to the fluorescence intensity. At pH values lower than 5, however, the surface groups begin to be protonated, which leads to the reduction of net surface charge and the formation of hydrogen bonds between the particles. As a result, the Nb2C QDs precipitate and their fluorescence intensity decreases. The fluorescence intensity of Nb2C QDs upon switching between acidic and basic environments was further studied. From Fig. 4c, it is clear that Nb2C QDs exhibit an excellent reversible fluorescence behavior, which confirms the robust chemical stability of Nb2C QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Potential of Nb2C QDs as fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para:
To explore the potential of Nb2C QDs as fluorescent probes, their metal ions sensing and imaging capacity were investigated in detail. Upon addition of 15 types of metal ions () that usually existed in biological system and natural water environment, [38,39] as shown in Fig. 5
a and Fig. 5b, it is evident that the fluorescence of Nb2C QDs can only be quenched by Fe3+ and almost no obvious response to other metal ions is observed, indicating excellent sensing selectivity of Nb2C QDs toward Fe3+. The anti-interference ability of Nb2C QDs for Fe3+ sensing is confirmed in Fig. 5c, where no difference in fluorescence quenching efficiency is observed upon mixing competitive ions with Fe3+. The high fluorescence detection selectivity of Nb2C QDs for Fe3+ can be attributed to the special coordination interaction between the Fe3+ ions and the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the Nb2C QDs, which has been widely used for the detection of Fe3+ or colored reactions in traditional organic chemistry [40–42].

To understand the Fe3+ quenching process of Nb2C QDs, the luminescence decay profile of Nb2C QDs was performed. As displayed in Fig. S2a, the average lifetime of Nb2C QDs is 1.51 ns, which is changed to 1.49 ns with the addition of 300 μM Fe3+. Judging from the relative large magnitude of the quenching constant (shown in Fig. 5e) and nearly constant FL lifetime, the fluorescence quenching provoked by Fe3+ is probably due to static quenching arising from the formation of a stable nonfluorescence complex between Nb2C QDs and Fe3+ [39,43]. To prove the possible quenching mechanism, UV–vis absorption, dynamic light scattering measurement and TEM study were performed. As shown in Fig. S2b, after the addition of 300 μM Fe3+, the absorption of the mixture increased sharply, which indicates that the Nb2C QDs complexed with Fe3+. Moreover, the increase in particle size (Fig. S2c) and the observation of aggregation in TEM image (Fig. S2d) proved that Fe3+ indeed complexed with Nb2C QDs. Therefore, the detection mechanism of Nb2C quantum dots toward Fe3+ can be attributed to the formation of Nb2C QDs/Fe3+ complex, which leads to nonradiative electron-transfer that involves partial transfer of an electron in the excited state to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+ [44,45]. The response time of Fe3+ quenching was investigated shown in Fig. S3. It indicates that the maximum fluorescence quenching efficiency can be achieved within 3 s of Fe3+ addition and remains essentially constant afterward. Such a fast quenching characteristic are advantageous for practical sensing applications of Nb2C QDs.

Before fluorescence imaging testing, the cytotoxicity of Nb2C QDs was measured via an MTT assay. As shown in Fig. 6 a, even at a concentration up to 200 μg mL−1, Nb2C QDs show negligible effect on cell viability, which implies that Nb2C QDs have good biocompatibility and potential for bioimaging. Fig. 6b-d shows the confocal laser fluorescence microscope images of cells after incubating with different concentrations of Nb2C QDs (100−200 μg mL−1) for 4 h. It is found that the cytoplasmic areas of cells are selectively stained by Nb2C QDs and emit intensively green fluorescence, indicating an excellent cytoplasmic localization of Nb2C QDs [26,46]. In addition, the clear observation of cell morphology demonstrates that Nb2C QDs possess promising application in cell imaging and labelling. Owing to their good biocompatibility, cell imaging property and Fe3+ sensing performance, the Nb2C QDs show promising applicability for monitoring intracellular Fe3+ levels. As illustrated in Fig. S4, with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ in cells, the fluorescence intensity was decreased step by step. This indicates that the Nb2C QDs can be used for sensing Fe3+ in the living cells.

It is well known that endowing implantable biomaterials with in vivo imaging ability is another important application of fluorescent species, and there has been an increasing demand for the development of such biomaterials recently [47–49]. To further demonstrate the potential of Nb2C QDs as fluorescent probes, a small animal in vivo fluorescence imaging system was used to investigate the fluorescent images of shaved mice with different shapes of poly(vinyl alcohol)/Nb2C QDs composite hydrogels subcutaneously implanted on their back. The composite hydrogels were facilely fabricated via a freezing/thawing approach according to our previous work [14]. As depicted in Fig. 6e-f, the fluorescence pseudo- colour images display the locations and intact shapes of the corresponding hydrogels and clear boundaries between the hydrogels and surrounding tissues are observed. These findings indicate that the Nb2C QDs are capable of fluorescence imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photostability and biodegradability of Nb2C QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Traditional fluorescent sensor and imaging agent are either easily photobleached or non-biodegradable; therefore, the development of novel fluorescent probes with both excellent photostability and biodegradability is of great significance for further widening their practical application. In this work, the photobleaching resistance of Nb2C QDs was investigated by continual irradiating under high intensity UV light. As shown in Fig. 7
a and Fig. 7b, after 40 min of 350 W xenon lamp irradiation, few detectable fluorescence quenching occurs in Nb2C QDs, while the representative organic fluorescent probes, fluorescein (FITC) and Rhodamine B (RhB), all exhibit severe photobleaching under the same conditions and their fluorescence intensity decreases by at least 20 % after 40 min exposure. These results demonstrate that Nb2C QDs have much better photostability compared to that of traditional fluorescent probes [46].

As emphasized in literatures, nanomaterials are easily enriched in tissues or organs, leading to adverse reactions and long-term toxicity [50,51]. Therefore, biodegradable property is favored by clinical nanoagents, ensuring that they can be excreted harmlessly from the body after fulfilling their functions [52]. Herein, inspired by the degradation of carbon-based nanomaterials by hypochlorous acid and reactive radical intermediates (generated from myeloperoxidase) [53–55], we investigated the biodegradation behavior of Nb2C QDs by human myeloperoxidase (hMPO). The degradation experiment was conducted by incubating Nb2C QDs in PBS solution containing both hMPO and H2O2. For comparison, Nb2C QDs were also incubated in the PBS solution with and without H2O2, respectively. Fig. 8
a shows digital photos of the abovementioned three samples of suspensions after incubating for 24 h. It is clear that the suspension sample containing hMPO and H2O2 become colorless and translucent while the other two control samples maintain the original yellow colour of Nb2C QDs, which implies the Nb2C QDs can be degraded via hMPO. Furthermore, the decrease of UV–vis absorption and fluorescence intensity also reflect the occurrence of degradation (Fig. 8b-c). AFM was used to analyze the morphology of Nb2C QDs after 24 h immersing in the three different solution conditions. For Nb2C QDs in pure PBS solution (Fig. 8d), their morphology and thickness barely exhibit any changes from those of the original Nb2C QDs (Fig. 2a), indicating that they are not degraded. In contrast, the Nb2C QDs treated in PBS/H2O2 mixture display an obvious reduction of their lateral dimensions, and drastically lose their characteristic 2D structures (Fig. 8e). This is probably due to the oxidation of Nb2C QDs by H2O2, and similar phenomena have been reported in literatures. [50] As shown in Fig. 8f, after incubation in PBS solution containing both hMPO and H2O2 for 24 h, the Nb2C QDs can no longer be found by AFM, which indicates that Nb2C QDs have been completely degraded. Overall, these data manifest that Nb2C QDs possess good biodegradation property responsive to hMPO.

In summary, novel Nb2C QDs are synthesized by a pulsed ultrasound assisted exfoliation in TPAOH. The intrinsic crystallographic structures of Nb2C MXene phases are well maintained in the ultrasmall Nb2C QDs. The obtained Nb2C QDs exhibit excellent chemical stability and biocompability, and their excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviors possess strong resistance to the photobleaching. In addition, the Nb2C QDs display great potential in fluorescence imaging and sensitive fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions. Remarkably, the obtained Nb2C QDs have been demonstrated to be enzymatic degradable with hMPO. Comparing to conventional nanofluorophores, the unique combination of excellent chemical stability, biocompatibility, anti-photobleaching ability and enzyme-responsive biodegradability of the as-prepared Nb2C QDs may render them as promising candidates for bioimaging, specific diagnose and sensing, visual display, and optical anti-counterfeiting applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21802097, 51778369, 21875144) and Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (Grant No. 2018A030313758, 2017A030310324, 2017A030313047).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127735.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guanghui Yang received his PhD from Sichuan University in 2014. He is currently a researcher in college of materials science and engineering, Shenzhen University. His research interests focus on fluorescent probes and functional nanomaterials.

Jinlai Zhao received his undergraduate education from Shenzhen University in 2014, and he is currently working for his Doctoral Degree in Shenzhen University. His research interests include the application of fluorescent and 2D materials.
Shaozhen Yi received his BS degree in 2018 and now is pursuing for MS degree in Shenzhen University. His research interests focus on fluorescent probes for metal ion sensing.

Xuejuan Wan received her PhD from University of Science and Technology of China in 2011. She is currently an associate professor in college of materials science and engineering, Shenzhen University. Her research interests focus on fluorescent probes and hybrid polymeric assemblies for sensing, imaging, and nanomedicine.

Jiaoning Tang received his PhD from Xi'an Jiaotong University in 1994. He is currently a professor in college of materials science and engineering, Shenzhen University. His research interests focus on the preparation of nanometer functional materials.
